passage: She was one of the most beautiful women in the county. The men in town called her Breedlove because of the way she had once loved a man — before the children, before the poverty, before the ugliness settled in her eyes. Her daughter Pecola prayed every night for blue eyes, certain that if she woke up beautiful everything would be different.

I had always felt uncomfortable with the labels others gave me. The word girl felt like a costume I hadn't chosen. When I first encountered the term nonbinary, something shifted — not a revelation exactly, but a recognition. Here, finally, was language that fit the shape of what I had always been.

Being Black and queer meant living at an intersection where neither community always made room for you. I had to build my own sense of belonging, piece by piece, from the memories of my grandmother's kitchen and the voices of writers who had survived by refusing to disappear. Brotherhood, I learned, is not given. It is chosen.

124 was spiteful. Full of a baby's venom. The women in the neighborhood called it unholy. Sethe had lived there eighteen years, sharing the house with the rage of her dead daughter — a rage so thick it could knock you sideways, scatter your shoes, and send mirrors sliding off walls.

I witnessed the whole thing. Khalil was unarmed. The officer told him to put his hands on the roof and Khalil turned back to check on me, just for a second, and then three shots rang out. I kept thinking: somebody is going to have to answer for this. That somebody was going to have to be me.

It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen. Winston Smith, his chin nuzzled into his breast in an effort to escape the vile wind, slipped quickly through the glass doors of Victory Mansions, though not quickly enough to prevent a swirl of gritty dust from entering along with him.

It was a pleasure to burn. It was a special pleasure to see things eaten, to see things blackened and changed. With the brass nozzle in his fists, with this great python spitting its venomous kerosene upon the world, the blood pounded in Montag's head, and his hands were the hands of some amazing conductor playing all the symphonies of blazing and burning.

passage: Atticus said to Jem one day, 'I'd rather you shoot at tin cans in the back yard, but I know you'll go after birds. Shoot all the bluejays you want, if you can hit 'em, but remember it's a sin to kill a mockingbird.' That was the only time I ever heard Atticus say it was a sin to do something.

My name is Offred. I have another name, which nobody uses now because it's forbidden. In Gilead, names are reassigned to erase who you were. I tell myself it doesn't matter — your name is like your telephone number, useful only to others. But what I tell myself is wrong — it does matter. I keep the name alive, in the back of my mind, the way you hoard something you might need someday.

I started sketching my father as a mouse and myself as a mouse and the Nazis as cats because that's what they were — predators, and we were prey. My father told me his stories haltingly, in pieces, the way survivors do. Each time he stopped, I wondered if the silence meant he had finally run out of words, or if some things could not be said in any language.

Billy Pilgrim has come unstuck in time. Billy has gone to sleep a senile widower and awakened on his wedding day. He has walked through a door in 1955 and come out another one in 1941. He has gone back through that door to find himself in 1963. So it goes.

Dear God, I am Celie, and I am fourteen years old. I have always been a good girl. Maybe you can give me a sign letting me know what is happening to me. My mama died when I was eight. My stepfather has lived with us since. He beats me, then he rapes me. Then he takes my baby. I don't know why he do this to me.

Dear friend, I am Charlie, and I am writing to you because she said you listen. I am what people call a wallflower — someone who watches and feels everything but never says it out loud. She said you understand, that you didn't try to sleep with that person at that party even though you could have. I just need to know that people like you exist. We are infinite, you and I.

I was sitting in my room thinking about the Great Perhaps, wondering if Alaska had found hers. She used to talk about escaping the labyrinth of suffering — that was the question she carried everywhere. She had spent so much of her life racing toward some imagined future, and I had spent so much of mine watching her, never close enough to understand what it was she was running from.

I was a reservation kid who decided to go to the white school. My parents were shocked. My sister was indifferent. But Rowdy, my best friend, looked at me like I had just said I was from another planet. 'You're going to Reardan?' he asked. 'That's twenty-two miles from here.' 'I know,' I said. 'I have to.' He didn't understand. I barely did either.

This is not a history book. It is a remix. It takes the tangled, three-hundred-year-old history of racist ideas in America and makes it accessible to anyone willing to look. There are three types of people in this world when it comes to race: racist, antiracist, and the kind of person who says they are neither — which, in truth, is always the first.

Coming out is not one moment. It is not a door you walk through and arrive changed. Being gay, or queer, or bi, or trans — each word carries its own weight and history. Coming out is more like a series of small daily decisions about who you trust with the truth of who you are. Some days you are out to everyone. Some days you are a secret even to yourself.

At camp, everyone gave you a nickname. Mine was Aiden, still, but some of the older boys had started calling me Flamer behind my back. I didn't understand what it meant at first. Then I understood too well. I learned to walk differently, to hold my voice flat, to take up less space. I practiced disappearing.

My name is Mike Muñoz. I'm twenty-two years old and I mow lawns for a living. Not because I dreamed of it — nobody dreams of this. I do it because I needed work and work was scarce and somewhere along the way I started to realize that what a man does for money doesn't have to define what he is capable of becoming.

In East Texas, in 1937, there were rules about who could love whom and what happened to people who broke those rules. Naomi knew them the way you know the shape of a scar — by feel, in the dark. Wash knew them too. They met anyway, by the river, where the trees were thick enough to hide two people who had no business being together.

There is a way that fathers and sons in Kabul talk around the things that matter. My father, Baba, was a man of great silences and greater acts. He built an orphanage with his own money and he never spoke of it. What I, Amir, did not know then — what I only understood years later, in America, too far from everything to change it — was that silence is not the same as peace.

I am Melinda, and I have been trying to remember who I was before the party — before IT happened. I used to talk constantly. My mother said I came out of the womb with opinions. Now the words sit in my chest like stones, too heavy to lift. I drew a picture of a tree in art class and my teacher, Mr. Freeman, thought it meant something. It did.

Over the main entrance the words, CENTRAL LONDON HATCHERY AND CONDITIONING CENTRE, and, in a shield, the World State's motto, COMMUNITY, IDENTITY, STABILITY. Inside, Bernard Marx moved between the rows of incubating embryos. Outside in his pocket, he carried soma tablets he would never take. My lousy childhood — and all that David Copperfield kind of crap — I don't feel like going into, if you want to know the truth. What I can tell you is that the world is full of phony people and I am very tired of them.

My father died in 1980, the same year I came out as a lesbian. I, Alison Bechdel, had not known he was gay, and he had not known I was gay, or if he did, he took that knowledge with him. The funeral home we called the Fun Home was the one he ran. What we shared was a love of literature and a talent for concealment, and his death reshaped everything I would ever write.

I, Maya Angelou, grew up knowing why the caged bird beats his wing till its blood is red on the cruel bars. The free bird thinks of another breeze and the trade winds soft through the sighing trees — but a caged bird sings of things unknown. Stamps, Arkansas was my cage. And still I sang.

My name is Callie, and I wanted to work on the musical more than anything — building the sets, designing the whole world of the play. But backstage, everything was more complicated than the stage. Jesse and his brother were both trying out, and one of them kissed a boy during rehearsal and nobody made a big deal of it. That surprised me. It also made me feel something I couldn't name yet.

Roy and Silo had been together for six years at the Central Park Zoo. They bowed to each other every morning. They swam together. They sang to each other in the way that chinstrap penguins do, a braying, joyful noise. When a keeper offered them an egg that needed tending, they took turns keeping it warm. The egg hatched, and they named her Tango.

The committee of Elders had named him Receiver of Memory — an Assignment unlike any other in the community. He was the one who would hold all the pain and beauty that Sameness had erased from everyone else's lives. He had not asked for this. He did not know yet what it would cost him.

Some things you do because you have to. Some things you do because you believe there is no other way. Sethe believed. That was the difference between her and everyone else.

Starr. That's my name. It's the kind of name that sounds like a joke until you meet me. I live in Garden Heights, which means I know how to move through a crowd at a block party and how to move through a hallway at Williamson Prep — two entirely different ways of being in a body. The night Khalil was shot, I was the only witness. That fact never left me alone.

Above them all, the face of Big Brother stared down from every wall.

He was a Black man accused by a white man's word, in a white man's court, before an all-white jury. Atticus Finch stood up in that courtroom anyway, and told the truth, and that was something nobody in Maycomb could forget or forgive.

My father, Vladek, was the most difficult man I ever loved. He saved every piece of string, every rubber band, every plastic bag — habits learned in Auschwitz, where he had survived by knowing how to make himself useful. After the war he carried that survival forward into peacetime. I, Art Spiegelman, drew him as a mouse and myself as a mouse and the tension between us as something that refused to fit into any panel.

The firebombing of Dresden happened in February 1945. I know because I was there, in a slaughterhouse, underground, with a hundred other American prisoners of war. When we came up from the meat locker the city was gone — a moonscape of rubble and ash. So it goes. I have tried many times to write about this and each time the words come out wrong, which is to say they come out true.

Shug Avery was the most beautiful thing I ever saw. Not just on the outside — though she was that too, in her silk dress and her red lipstick. She looked at me like I was a person. Like I was worth looking at. Celie, she said, and she said it like it was a whole sentence. We were in Georgia and the world was small and mean, but in that moment it opened up a little.

They played The Smiths in the car. They drove through a tunnel and Sam stood in the back of the pickup and the wind came in and she looked like everything good I ever imagined.

He said that was his answer too. We thought about Alaska every day for a long time after that, and wondered whether it had been an accident.

I'm not the hero of this story. I'm just a kid from the Spokane reservation who decided to leave. Every morning I woke up at five-thirty to ride a school bus twenty-two miles to Reardan, a white town, because the only way off the reservation was through education and education was in short supply where I was from. The kids at Reardan called me the Nomad. I called myself Junior. It's complicated.

Let's be honest: America has always had a race problem. Ibram X. Kendi and Jason Reynolds argue that there are only two positions — racist or antiracist — and that neutrality is a myth. This book shows you the racist ideas that shaped American history and the antiracist thinkers who fought back. It shows you where you stand, whether you are ready to look or not.

He would never tell me where the kite would fall — he always just knew.

She looked at him as if he were speaking another language. He was.

I was surrounded by phony people who never thought about ducks or anything real. That's what it was like — caring too much about the wrong things, which turned out to be all the right things.

I, Clay Jensen, found the shoebox in the mailroom. Inside were cassette tapes — each one numbered in nail polish. Hannah Baker had left instructions: listen, then pass them on. So I listened to Hannah Baker's voice, which was just like I remembered it, explain why I was on her list. Some of what she said I understood. Some of it I will never stop trying to understand.

They called me Detroit Red. Then the Nation of Islam found me in Norfolk Prison Colony and I began to read, and reading remade me. Brother Malcolm became Minister Malcolm.

I am an invisible man. No, I am not a spook like those who haunted Edgar Allan Poe; nor am I one of your Hollywood-movie ectoplasms. I am a man of substance, of flesh and bone, fiber and liquids — and I might even be said to possess a mind. I am invisible, understand, simply because people refuse to see me. They see only my surroundings, themselves, or figments of their imagination — indeed, everything and anything except me.

Fear and shame drove him on, not hate — though hate was in it too.

What she found was the village smug, satisfied, and suspicious of the arts. The Thanatopsis Club met once a month to improve itself. Nothing much improved. Carol Kennicott was considered a problem.

He was told he was under arrest but could continue his usual life. He never discovered the charge against him. The courts were in the attics of tenement buildings and justice was conducted in rooms full of sawdust.
